question_text: A student heats ice from -10°C to steam at 110°C. During this process, the mass stays constant at 50 g. The heating curve shows a long flat section between 0°C and 100°C. Which statement best explains why the temperature does not increase during melting and boiling?

options.label: A
options.text: The energy is being used to break bonds between particles so they can move more freely, rather than to increase temperature

options.label: B
options.text: The particles stop moving during melting and boiling so no kinetic energy is transferred

options.label: C
options.text: All the thermal energy escapes as heat into the surroundings during these phase changes

options.label: D
options.text: The particles are too heavy to move faster once they reach 0°C or 100°C

correct_answer: A) The energy is being used to break bonds between particles so they can move more freely, rather than to increase temperature
explanation: During melting and boiling, energy goes into overcoming forces between particles (latent heat) rather than increasing their kinetic energy, so temperature stays constant. Temperature only increases when particles move faster, which happens when energy increases their kinetic energy rather than breaking apart the structure.
